title: Divinity candy
subtitle: Meringue-based Southern American sugar candy
excerpt: A Southern American confection made by streaming hot sugar syrup cooked to hard ball stage into stiffly beaten egg whites. The result is a glossy, cloud-like candy that dries to a matte crust on the outside while staying soft inside. It requires a sugar thermometer and a stand mixer.
sourceNotes: Divinity candy is a Southern American confection with origins in the early twentieth century, most closely associated with the American South and often made at Christmas. It is a cooked meringue candy in the tradition of Italian meringue and nougat: hot sugar syrup is streamed into beaten egg whites to cook them and create a stable, firm candy. The recipe follows the standard American method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Divinity keeps at room temperature in an airtight tin for up to 1 week. It does not keep well in humid conditions: the candy absorbs moisture from the air and becomes sticky.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven; sugar syrup cooked to hard ball stage (130 degrees C) on the hob

glossaryTerms.definition: The key technique in divinity is streaming boiling sugar syrup into beaten egg whites while the mixer runs. Pour in a thin, steady stream down the side of the bowl, not directly onto the beaters, or the syrup will spin into threads on contact rather than incorporating. The egg whites cook from the heat of the syrup: this produces a stable foam that dries to a candy texture. The mixer must not stop during the pour.

content.text: Divinity is made by the same principle as Italian meringue: hot syrup cooked and then streamed into egg whites. The
requires two things happening at once, so a stand mixer is not optional. Divinity does not set on a humid day: make it when the weather is dry.
Cook the syrup and beat the whites

Combine 400 g granulated sugar, 100 g glucose syrup, and 120 ml water in a saucepan. Stir over low heat to dissolve the sugar, then stop stirring and bring to a boil. Cook to 130 degrees C. While the syrup is cooking, begin beating 2 room-temperature egg whites with 0.25 tsp salt in the stand mixer to stiff peaks. When the syrup reaches 130 degrees C, pour it in a thin, steady stream down the side of the mixing bowl with the mixer on high speed. Add 1 tsp vanilla. Continue beating on high for 6 to 8 minutes until the mixture is very thick, glossy, and holds its shape.

Working quickly, drop spoonfuls of the mixture onto baking paper. The mixture will firm as it cools and develop a matte crust. Leave for at least 1 hour to set before handling. The centres will remain soft.

items.problem: Divinity will not firm up and stays sticky
items.solution: The syrup did not reach the correct temperature, or the humidity is too high. Make divinity on a dry day. If the syrup was at temperature, the mixture may need more beating: continue for another 3 to 5 minutes.

items.problem: Sugar syrup crystallised in the pan
items.solution: The syrup was stirred after coming to a boil, or crystals on the pan sides fell back in. Do not stir after dissolving. Brush down the pan sides with a wet pastry brush before the syrup reaches temperature.
